next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Nate spent the first six workdays of Dominic Altieri's life calling in sick from a flip phone he still used like it might explode.

By Thursday Ray had stopped sounding sympathetic. By Friday he had stopped pretending this was funny. Monday morning, parked outside Kassos Development with Dominic's key ring sweating in his hand, Nate listened to Ray's last voicemail again because there was nothing else to do except go inside.

Come in or don't, Ray had said. But if you don't, don't make me keep lying for you.

The office occupied the second floor of a brick warehouse that had once held machine parts or textiles or whatever else old American buildings held before someone stripped the guts out and called the result loft-style. A rusted loading door still sat on the side of the building like an old scar. Somebody at Kassos had leaned into the history just enough to make it decorative. The lobby had exposed brick, black-and-white photos of tugboats on the harbor, and a potted ficus that had gone gray at the tips from office air and neglect. Up on the second floor the effect gave out. Carpet tile, fluorescent panels, beige desktop towers under desks, the hot dust smell of computers that had been on since dawn.

He sat in the Civic for another minute with the engine off, listening to the metal tick as it cooled. The July heat pressed through the windshield and turned the car into a damp box. Dominic's shirt stuck to his back. Sarah had watched him knot the tie that morning, then stepped in and redid it without comment, fingers quick and practiced at his throat. She ha...
open_hooks_to_carry: Navigating 2005 without GPS — the Rand McNally atlas, the physical strangeness of the pre-smartphone world
The strip mall: first sight of Eastbridge Plaza intact, alive, functioning
prev_tail_excerpt:
On the next line he wrote:

Sarah

Ray

Tommy

Ma

Kassos

The names from the phone, the morning, the edges of the map.

Under that he wrote, in smaller letters because somehow that felt safer:

Listen more.

Do not volunteer.

Search everything.

He stared at the phrase do not for a second, hearing how stiff it sounded even in his own head, and scratched over it until it became don't. The correction irritated him disproportionately, maybe because it was proof that even alone, even on paper, he could still sound like somebody pretending. He put the pen down and flexed Dominic's fingers until the cramp eased.

The apartment around him was no longer a crime scene, not exactly. Crime scenes were inert. This place was active. The phone could ring any minute. Someone could knock. Sarah could come back because she had forgotten something and find him standing over Dominic's paperwork like a thief in daylight. Every object in the room held information, and every object in the room could also expose him if he mishandled it.

Nate looked toward the bedroom where the unmade bed still held the hollow of two bodies.

Then he picked up the flip phone, opened it with his thumb, and started learning how to be the dead man he had made.
state_deltas_required: Nate's need to understand his new geography — and the gravitational pull of the strip mall where his father is alive
Nate sees Mort alive for the first time in nineteen years. The abstraction of revenge gives way to the embodied reality of a living father he cannot approach.
